Suppose we have a system of congruences with different moduli:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ b_1 \bmod n_1 \\ x ≡ b_2 \bmod n_2 \\ \vdots \\ x ≡ b_k \bmod n_k \end{gather}$$

And suppose \((n_i, n_j) = 1\) whenever \(i \ne j\). This means all the moduli are pairwise relatively prime. Let \(N = n_1n_2 \ldots n_k\) and let \(N_i = \frac{N}{n_i}\). \(N_i\) is basically the product of all the moduli except for \(n_i\).

Suppose \(h\), \(i\) and \(j\) are integers \(\le k\) and \(\ge 1\), but not equal to each other. If \((n_j,n_i)=1\) and \((n_k,n_i)=1\), then \((n_k n_j,n_i)=1\). We can repeated do this with all other moduli to show that the product of all moduli excluding \(n_i\) is relatively prime with \(n_i\). In other words, \((N_i, n_i) = 1\).

Since \((N_i,n_i)=1\), then there exists an inverse of \(N_i\) modulo \(n_i\), and we will denote this inverse as \(N_i^{-1}\).

The solution of the congruences is:

$$ x = N_1N_1^{-1}b_1 + N_2N_2^{-1}b_2 + \ldots + N_kN_k^{-1}b_k $$

This solution is unique modulo \(N\). This theorem is called the Chinese remainder theorem.

The Chinese remainder theorem can also be generalized where the moduli are not relatively coprime, but we will delve more into this in the exercises.

Find the least nonnegative solution for this system of congruences:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ 2 \bmod 3 \\ x ≡ 1 \bmod 4 \\ x ≡ 3 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

Let \(N = (3)(4)(5) = 60\), \(N_1 = 20\), \(N_2 = 15\) and \(N_3 = 12\). There exists inverses \(N_i^{-1}\):

$$ \begin{gather} 20 N_1^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 3 \implies N_1^{-1} = 2 \\ 15 N_2^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 4 \implies N_2^{-1} = 3 \\ 12 N_3^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \implies N_3^{-1} = 3\end{gather}$$

So the solution is:

$$\begin{align} x &= N_1N_1^{-1} (2) + N_2N_2^{-1} (1) + N_3N_3^{-1} (3) \\ &= 233 \end{align} $$

This means least nonnegtaive solution mod \(N\) would be \(x = 53\).

Find the least nonnegative solution for this system of congruences:

$$ \begin{gather} x ≡ 1 \bmod 7 \\ x ≡ 4 \bmod 6 \\ x ≡ 3 \bmod 5 \end{gather} $$

Let \(N = (7)(6)(5) = 210\), \(N_1 = 30\), \(N_2 = 35\) and \(N_3 = 42\). There exists inverses \(N_i^{-1}\):

$$ \begin{gather} 30 N_1^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 7 \implies N_1^{-1} = 4 \\ 35 N_2^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 6 \implies N_2^{-1} = 5 \\ 42 N_3^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \implies N_3^{-1} = 3\end{gather} $$

So the solution is:

$$\begin{align} x &= N_1N_1^{-1} (1) + N_2N_2^{-1} (4) + N_3N_3^{-1} (3) \\ &= 1198 \end{align} $$

This means least nonnegtaive solution mod \(N\) would be \(x = 148\).
Try to explain why the Chinese remainder theorem works.
FETCH:number_theory/more_congruence/Chinese_remainder_theorem

Find the least nonnegative solution for this system of congruences:

$$\begin{gather} 3x ≡ 2 \bmod 4 \\ 4x ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \\ 6x ≡ 3 \bmod 9 \end{gather}$$

Let's focus on \(6x ≡ 3 \bmod 9\) first. This means there should exist should \(y\) where:

$$ 6x-3 = 9y \implies 2x-1 = 3y $$

This means:

$$ 2x ≡ 1 \bmod 3 $$

The congruences we need to solve is:

$$\begin{gather} 3x ≡ 2 \bmod 4 \\ 4x ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \\ 2x ≡ 1 \bmod 3 \end{gather}$$

We need them to be in the form of \(x ≡ b \bmod m\), so we can do this:

$$\begin{gather} 3^{-1}3x ≡ 3^{-1}2 \bmod 4 \implies x ≡ 3^{-1}2 \bmod 4 \\ 4^{-1}4x ≡ 4^{-1}1 \bmod 5 \implies x ≡ 4^{-1}1 \bmod 5 \\ 2^{-1}2x ≡ 2^{-1}1 \bmod 3 \implies x ≡ 2^{-1}1 \bmod 3 \end{gather}$$

3-1 modulo 4 is 3, 4-1 modulo 5 is 4 and 2-1 modulo 3 is 2. This means:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ 6 \bmod 4 \\ x ≡ 4 \bmod 5 \\ x ≡ 2 \bmod 3 \end{gather}$$

Let \(N = (4)(5)(3) = 60\), \(N_1 = 15\), \(N_2 = 12\) and \(N_3 = 20\). There exists inverses \(N_i^{-1}\):

$$ \begin{gather} 15 N_1^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 4 \implies N_1^{-1} = 1 \\ 12 N_2^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \implies N_2^{-1} = 1 \\ 20 N_3^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 9 \implies N_3^{-1} = 5\end{gather} $$

So the solution is:

$$\begin{align} x &= N_1N_1^{-1} (6) + N_2N_2^{-1} (4) + N_3N_3^{-1} (5) \\ &= 494 \end{align} $$

This means least nonnegtaive solution mod \(N\) is \(x = 14\).

There were 1200 troops at the start of the battle. After the battle, the general lined them up 5 at a time and saw that there were 3 left over. He lined them up again 6 at a time and saw 3 left over again. He then lined them up 7 at a time and saw 1 left over. Finally, he lined them up 11 at a time and saw none left over. How many soldiers were there after the battle.

We need to find a solution for this system of congruences:

$$ x ≡ 3 \bmod 5 \\ x ≡ 3 \bmod 6 \\ x ≡ 1 \bmod 7 \\ x ≡ 0 \bmod 11 $$

Let \(N = 5*6*7*11 = 2310\), and let's define these constants:

$$\begin{gather} N_1 = \frac{N}{5} = 6*7*11 = 462 \\ N_2 = \frac{N}{6} = 5*7*11 = 385 \\ N_3 = \frac{N}{7} = 5*6*11 = 330 \\ N_4 = \frac{N}{11} = 5*6*7 = 210 \end{gather}$$

Since \((N_1,5)=1\), then an inverse of \(N_1\) modulo 5 exists:

$$\begin{gather} N_1 N_1^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \\ 462 N_1^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

Similarly:

$$\begin{gather} 385 N_2^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 6 \\ 330 N_3^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 7 \\ 210 N_4^{-1} ≡ 1 \bmod 11 \end{gather}$$

If we solve for each one:

$$\begin{gather} N_1^{-1} = 3, \quad N_2^{-1} = 1 \\ N_3^{-1} = 1, \quad N_4^{-1} = 1 \end{gather}$$

Which means one possible solution is:

$$\begin{gather} N_1 N_1^{-1} (3) + N_2 N_2^{-1} (1) + \\ N_3 N_3^{-1} (1) + N_4 N_4^{-1} (0) = 5643 \end{gather} $$

The solution is unique modulo \(N\), and is less than 1200, then we keep subtracting \(N\) until we get a positive integer less than 1200. Since \(5643-2N = 1023\), then the total count of the troops is 1023.

If eggs are removed from a basket 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 at a time, there remain, respectively, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 eggs. If the eggs are removed 7 at a time, then no eggs remain. What is the least number of eggs.

We need to solve these system of congruences:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ 1 \bmod 2 \\ x ≡ 2 \bmod 3 \\ x ≡ 3 \bmod 4 \\ x ≡ 4 \bmod 5 \\ x ≡ 5 \bmod 6 \\ x ≡ 0 \bmod 7 \end{gather}$$

Let's focus on the first five:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ 1 \bmod 2 \\ x ≡ 2 \bmod 3 \\ x ≡ 3 \bmod 4 \\ x ≡ 4 \bmod 5 \\ x ≡ 5 \bmod 6 \end{gather}$$

We can rewrite them as:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ -1 \bmod 2 \\ x ≡ -1 \bmod 3 \\ x ≡ -1 \bmod 4 \\ x ≡ -1 \bmod 5 \\ x ≡ -1 \bmod 6 \end{gather}$$

This means \(x+1\) is a multiple of 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6. The lcm of 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 is 60, which means:

$$\begin{gather}x+1=60k \\ x = 60k -1\end{gather}$$

We also know that \(x\) or \(60k-1\) is a multiple of 7. If we manually try \(k=1\), we get \(60k-1=59\) which is not divisible by 7. If we manually try \(k=2\), we get \(60k-1=119\), which is a multiple of 7. This means \(x=119\) is a valid solution.
What is the smallest number of lobsters in a tank if 1 lobster is left over when they are removed 2, 3, 5 or 7 at a time, but no lobsters are left over when they are removed 11 at a time.

If \(x\) be the number of lobsters:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ 1 \bmod 2 \\ x ≡ 1 \bmod 3 \\ x ≡ 1 \bmod 5 \\ x ≡ 1 \bmod 7 \\ x ≡ 0 \bmod 11 \end{gather}$$

This means \(x-1\) is a multiple of 2, 3, 5 and 7, and also that \(x\) is a multiple of 11. The lcm of 2, 3, 5 and 7 is 210, then \(x-1 = 210k\). Also, \(x=11h\). Therefore, \(11h - 1= 210k\), or \(11h-210k=1\). Since \(11(-19)-210(-1)=1\), then \(h=-19\) and \(k=-1\).

This means one possible solution for \(x\) is -209. Since each moduli is paiswise relatively prime, then the solution is congruent modulo \(2*3*5*7*11=2310\). This means the smallest positive solution for \(x\) is \(-209+2310=2101\).

Prove that the system of congruences:

$$\begin{gather}x ≡ a_1 mod m_1 \\ x ≡ a_2 mod m_2\end{gather}$$

has a solution if and only if:

$$ (m_1, m_2)|(a_1 - a_2) $$

and that the solution is unique modulo \([m_1,m_2]\).
FETCH:number_theory/more_congruence/generalChineseRemainderTheoremWhereModuliNonCoprimeBaseCase

Solve the system of congruences:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ 10 mod 60 \\ x ≡ 80 mod 350 \end{gather}$$

Find the smallest positive solution.

Since \((60,350)|(80-10)\), then a solution exists.

We can rewrite them as:

$$\begin{gather} 60s = x - 10 \\ 350t = x - 80\end{gather}$$

Making \(x\) the subject:

$$\begin{gather} 60s + 10 = 350t + 80 \\ 6s + 1 = 35t + 8 \\ 6s-35t=7 \end{gather}$$

Since \((6,35)=1\), then we can use the Euclidean algorithm:

$$\begin{gather} 6p + 35q = 1 \\ \therefore q=-1, p=6 \end{gather}$$

We can multiply both sides by 7:

$$\begin{gather} 6(6)(7) + 35(-1)(7) = 7 \end{gather}$$

This means \(s=42\) and \(t=7\):

$$\begin{gather} 9 = 35t-6s \\ \implies 9 = 35(42)-6(-7) \end{gather}$$

Since \(60s=x-10\), then \(x=2530\). This holds true in the other equation as well: \(350t = x - 80\).

The solution is congruent modulo \([60,250]=2100\). Therefore, the solution is \(2530-2100=430\).

Solve the system of congruences:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ 2 mod 910 \\ x ≡ 93 mod 1001 \end{gather}$$

Find the smallest positive solution.

From the congruences, we can say:

$$\begin{gather} 910s =x-2 \\ 1001t = x-93 \end{gather}$$

Rearranging:

$$\begin{gather} 910s+2=x=1001t+93 \\ 910s-1001t = 91 \end{gather}$$

Since \((910,1001)=91\), then \(t=-1\) and \(s=-1\). Which means one possible solution is \(x=-1001+93=-908\). Since \([910,1001]=10010\), then the smallest positive solution is \(10010-908 = 9102\)

Prove the general Chinese remainder theorem, that says that a system of (k+1\) congruences shown below:

$$\begin{gather}\begin{gather} x ≡ a_1 \bmod m_1 \\ x ≡ a_2 \bmod m_2 \\ \vdots \\ x ≡ a_k \bmod m_k \end{gather} \end{gather}$$

will have a solution if and only if \((m_i,m_j) | a_i - a_j\) whenever \(i \ne j\), and that the solution is unique modulo \([m_1,\ldots, m_k]\).

You can use induction here.
FETCH:number_theory/more_congruence/generalChineseRemainderTheoremWhereModuliNonCoprime

Solve the system of congruences:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ 5 \bmod 6 \\ x ≡ 3 \bmod 10 \\ x ≡ 8 \bmod 15 \end{gather}$$

We know that this is solvable because \((15,10)|8-3\), \((15,6)|8-5\) and \((10,6)|5-3\). Let's try to solve the first two congruences first:

$$\begin{gather} x ≡ 5 \bmod 6 \\ x ≡ 3 \bmod 10 \end{gather}$$

This means:

$$\begin{gather} 6k = x-5 \\ 10h = x-3 \\ 6k+5 = 10h+3 \\ 2 = 10h - 6k \\ 1 = 5h - 3k \end{gather}$$

This means \(h=2\) and \(k=3\), which means \(x=23\). That means 23 is the solution to the first two congruences, and since \([6,10]=30\), the solution is unique modulo 30. Now we need a solution for all three congruences. To do that, we can just find the value of \(x\) such that \(x≡23 \bmod 30\) (so it solves the first two congruences) and \(x ≡ 8 \bmod 15\) (for the last congruence)

$$\begin{gather} x≡23 \bmod 30 \\ x ≡ 8 \bmod 15 \end{gather}$$

Now our task is to solve these two congruences. We can rewrite them as:

$$\begin{gather} 30s = x-23 \\ 15t = x-8 \\ 30s +23 = 15t + 8 \\ 30s - 15t = -15 \\ 2s-t = -1 \end{gather}$$

Therefore, \(s=1\) and \(t=3\), this means \(x=53\). The solution for the two congruences is unique modulo \([30,15]=30\). This means the smallest positive integer for \(x\) is \(53-30=23\).
